# Moments, Existence, and Inefficiency Determinants {#sec-existence}

```{r}
#| label: setup-03
#| include: false
#| cache: false
library(countSFA)
library(ggplot2)
library(kableExtra)
options(knitr.table.format = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "latex" else "html")
```

We show in this section that the marginal moments of $Y_i$ may fail to exist for small $b$, while the log-likelihood, the conditional efficiency score, and the MLE remain well-defined throughout the parameter space. More specifically:

(i) The marginal PMFs @eq-pmf-gamma and @eq-pmf-gamma-cost (and hence the log-likelihood) are well-defined for all
$b > 0$, with no restriction. 
(ii) The conditional moments $\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$, for $r \in \mathbb{R}$, that underlie the technical efficiency scores @eq-te-gamma and @eq-te-gamma-cost are finite in both orientations, regardless of $b$.
(iii) The unconditional moments of $Y_i$ exist for all $b > 0$ in the production case, but require $b > 1$ for the
mean and $b > 2$ for the variance in the cost case. 

Only (iii) imposes a restriction on $b$, and only when reporting the marginal moments of the dependent variable. the MGF of the Gamma distribution evaluated at $t = -1$ and $t = -2$ gives

$$
\mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i}] \;=\; \left(\frac{b}{b+1}\right)^{\!\alpha},
\qquad
\mathrm{E}[e^{-2 u_i}] \;=\; \left(\frac{b}{b+2}\right)^{\!\alpha},
$$ {#eq-mgf-prod}

both arguments negative, so these moments exist for all $b > 0$. Combining with the conditional Poisson variance via the variance decomposition yields, in the production frontier,

$$
\mathrm{E}[Y_i]
\;=\; \lambda_i \!\left(\frac{b}{b+1}\right)^{\!\alpha},
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}[Y_i]
\;=\; \mathrm{E}[Y_i]
  + \lambda_i^2\!\left[\!\left(\frac{b}{b+2}\right)^{\!\alpha}
    - \left(\frac{b}{b+1}\right)^{\!2\alpha}\right].
$$ {#eq-moments-gamma}

The variance-to-mean ratio strictly exceeds one at every parameter value: the bracketed term is always positive as $b(b+1)^2 > b^2(b+2)$ implies $b^2 + 2b +1 > b^2 + 2b)$. The Gamma frontier is therefore unconditionally  overdispersed relative to the Poisson, with the additional variability attributable to heterogeneous inefficiency growing quadratically in the frontier mean $\lambda_i$. The bracketed term vanishes as $b \to \infty$ (no inefficiency) or $\alpha \to \infty$ at fixed mean $\alpha/b$ (degenerate inefficiency). Setting $\alpha = 1$ recovers the exponential moments

$$
\mathrm{E}[Y_i] \;=\; \lambda_i\,\frac{b}{b+1},
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}[Y_i] \;=\; \lambda_i\,\frac{b}{b+1}
  + \lambda_i^2\,\frac{b}{(b+1)^2(b+2)},
$$ {#eq-moments}

both visible in the left panel of @fig-pmf-shape as the leftward shift and quadratic left-tail growth of the distribution as $b$ decreases.

The Poisson limit is likewise visible as the grey reference bars in both panels of @fig-pmf-shape, which the $b = 5$ (resp.\ $\alpha = 4$) curves closely approximate. As $b \to 0$ the mean inefficiency $\alpha/b$ diverges,
$\mathrm{E}[Y_i] \to 0$, and all units approach zero output.

In the cost frontier the analogous formulas use the Gamma MGF at $t = \lbrace 1, 2\rbrace$ and imply:

$$
\mathrm{E}[Y_i] \;=\; \lambda_i\!\left(\frac{b}{b-1}\right)^{\!\alpha}
\quad(\text{requires } b > 1),
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}[Y_i]\quad(\text{requires } b > 2).
$$ {#eq-moments-cost}

The first of these conditions is the one [@feHofler2013, fn. 7] flags for the half-normal cost frontier. The next subsection shows that this is the only restriction $b > 1$ imposes on the Gamma frontier: the log-likelihood and the limit of the estimator of the efficiency scores remain well-defined for any $b > 0$ even in the cost frontier.

## PMF, Likelihood, and Conditional Moment Existence

The PMFs @eq-pmf-gamma and @eq-pmf-gamma-cost are well-defined for all $b > 0$: in each orientation a factor that decays faster than any exponential dominates the polynomial growth from $u^{\alpha-1}$, ensuring absolute convergence of
the alternating series (production) or finiteness of the integrand (cost) with no restriction on $b$. The detailed argument is given in Appendix. Because the log-likelihood depends only on the PMF, it inherits well-definedness on the entire interior of the parameter space. Under the regularity conditions of @neweyMcFadden1994 [Theorems 2.5 and 3.3], which hold whenever $\alpha$ is bounded away from zero and $b$ is bounded away from zero and infinity, the MLE is consistent and $\sqrt{n}$-asymptotically normal.

## Conditional moments and the efficiency score

The conditional efficiency scores @eq-te-gamma and @eq-te-gamma-cost are conditional moments of $e^{-u_i}$ given $y_i$, not unconditional moments of the Gamma random variable. Conditioning on $y_i$ inserts a Poisson-likelihood factor into the posterior density that strictly enlarges the set of $r$ for which $\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ is finite. The posterior densities are

$$
f(u_i \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i) \;\propto\; u_i^{\alpha - 1}\,
   \exp\!\bigl(-(b + y_i)\,u_i\bigr)\,
   \exp\!\bigl(-\lambda_i e^{-u_i}\bigr),\qquad u_i > 0,
$$ {#eq-post-prod}

in the production frontier, and

$$
f(u_i \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i) \;\propto\; u_i^{\alpha - 1}\,
   \exp\!\bigl((y_i - b)\,u_i\bigr)\,
   \exp\!\bigl(-\lambda_i e^{u_i}\bigr),
$$ {#eq-post-cost}

in the cost frontier. The conditional moment $\mathrm{E}[e^{r u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ is finite if and only if $r < b + y_i$ in the production case, which holds for $r = -1$ and any $b > 0$ and $y_i \geq 0$, and is finite for every $r \in \mathbb{R}$ in the cost case, regardless of $b$. The cost-frontier efficiency score is therefore
well-defined for all $b > 0$, in marked contrast to the unconditional MGF $\mathrm{E}[e^{u_i}] = (b/(b-1))^\alpha$ which requires $b > 1$. @sec-app-condmoments provides the detailed argument: the non-existence of the unconditional moment when $b \leq 1$ does not propagate to the conditional, because the Poisson likelihood supplies additional damping in the right tail of $u_i$.

::: {.remark title="Practical implication"}
Estimation and efficiency scoring are unaffected by the value of $\hat b$: the log-likelihood and the conditional expectation $\mathrm{E}[e^{\pm u_i}\mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ are finite for every $b > 0$ in both orientations. The restriction $b > 1$ matters only when researchers wish to report the cost-frontier marginal mean $\mathrm{E}[Y_i]$ in closed form.
:::

## Inefficiency Determinants: The Scaling Model {#sec-scaling}

The homogeneous model treats all units as drawing from the same inefficiency distribution. In practice, firm-level characteristics may systematically shift the level of inefficiency, and restricting all units to the same distribution conflates genuine inefficiency with observable heterogeneity.

The scaling (or multiplicative-heteroscedastic) frontier model of @caudillFord1993, @hadri1999, and @wangSchmidt2002, which generalises the panel-frontier formulation of @batteseCoelli1995, accommodates such
inefficiency determinants by writing $u_i = g(\mathbf{z}_i, \boldsymbol{\delta}) \cdot u^*$ where $u^* \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, b)$ and $\mathbf{z}_i$ is a vector of observed inefficiency drivers with parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ to be estimated jointly
with $\boldsymbol{\beta}$. This implies $u_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, b_i)$ with a unit-specific rate

$$
b_i \;=\; \frac{b}{g(\mathbf{z}_i,\, \boldsymbol{\delta})}.
$$ {#eq-scaling-rate}

The standard choice $g(\mathbf{z}_i, \boldsymbol{\delta}) = \exp(\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta})$ guarantees $b_i > 0$ for all values of $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ and yields the log-linear mean-inefficiency structure

$$
b_i \;=\; b\,\exp\!\left(-\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta}\right),
\qquad
\mathrm{E}(u_i) \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,\exp(\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta})}{b},
$$ {#eq-scaling-bi}

so that $b_i \to \infty$ (full efficiency) as $\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta} \to -\infty$ and $b_i \to 0$ (complete inefficiency) as $\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta} \to +\infty$.

A useful property of the count-data frontier is that the closed-form structure of @prp-pmf and @prp-te survives the introduction of scaling without modification. Because both the production-frontier alternating series @eq-pmf-gamma and the cost-frontier integral @eq-pmf-gamma-cost depend on $b$ only as a scalar, they hold observation-by-observation with $b$ replaced by $b_i$. In the exponential case ($\alpha = 1$) the production log-likelihood becomes

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}, b, \boldsymbol{\delta})
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^n \Bigl[
  \log b_i - b_i a_i + \log \gamma\!\left(y_i + b_i,\; e^{a_i}\right)
  - \log y_i!
\Bigr],
$$ {#eq-loglik-scaling}

evaluated exactly at every parameter value via the lower incomplete gamma function. For general $\alpha$ the production log-likelihood retains the alternating-series form @eq-pmf-gamma with $b \mapsto b_i$, and the cost-frontier log-likelihood retains its one-dimensional integral form.

The conditional efficiency score inherits the same analytic structure. In the exponential production case, the same substitution used to prove @cor-te-exp gives

$$
\mathrm{E}\!\left[e^{-u_i} \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i,\, \mathbf{z}_i\right]
\;=\; e^{-a_i} \cdot
  \frac{\gamma\!\left(y_i + b_i + 1,\; e^{a_i}\right)}
       {\gamma\!\left(y_i + b_i,\; e^{a_i}\right)},
$$ {#eq-te-scaling}

identical in form to @eq-te-formula with $b$ replaced by the unit-specific $b_i = b\,\exp(-\mathbf{z}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta})$. The variables $\mathbf{z}_i$ enter the efficiency score only through this substitution. Analogous expressions hold for the
cost-frontier exponential case (via @eq-te-formula-cost), the production-frontier Gamma case (via @eq-te-gamma), and the cost-frontier Gamma case (via @eq-te-gamma-cost), each with $b \mapsto b_i$.

The null hypothesis of no inefficiency determinants is $H_0{:}\;\boldsymbol{\delta} = \mathbf{0}$, testable via a likelihood ratio test comparing the scaling and homogeneous models; under $H_0$ the test statistic is asymptotically $\chi^2$ with $\dim(\boldsymbol{\delta})$ degrees of freedom. Identification is cleanest when the variables in $\mathbf{z}_i$ are excluded from
$\mathbf{x}_i$: if the same variable enters both the frontier mean and the scaling function, identification of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ relies on the nonlinear entry of $b_i$ into the PMF rather than on an
exclusion restriction.

With the PMF, efficiency score, moments, and scaling extension all in hand, @sec-estimation builds the log-likelihood and describes how numerical optimisation is conducted, including the treatment of starting values and
the avoidance of degenerate parameter paths on the boundary of the parameter space.
